title: SFMC Tips #290 : Marketing Cloud Next: Personalization with Content Variables
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--18ebb6764911---------------------------------------)

6 min read

May 11, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*grbnaELzFwvCHpm2)

Photo by [Zhang Yi](https://unsplash.com/@mianmian1997?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

In the [Summer ’26 new feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-summer-26-release-highlights-04f6c5abdee6) for **Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions**, it became possible to add merge fields to messages based on a new data source called **“Content Variables”.**

When marketers hear the term **“Content Variables”,** it may sound a little unfamiliar at first. However, in my understanding, it is easier to imagine them as something similar to **“Personalization Strings”** in **Marketing Cloud Engagement** that are defined and used as needed.

Of course, Marketing Cloud Next also supports personalization through **Data Graphs**, but one of the key characteristics of **Content Variables** is that values can be retrieved and utilized in a more real-time manner.

In a previous article, when testing the scenario **“Case Closed ⇒ Immediately Send a Personalized Email to the Contact”,** I adopted a structure where an **“Apex-Defined” variable** was created, and values were passed to the content side using an **Apex Class Data Provider**.

However, this method was a **developer-centric approach**, which made it somewhat difficult for marketers to handle.

With the **“Content Variables”** feature added in the **Summer ’26** new feature release, this entire process can now be implemented **without Apex**, enabling **low-code personalization**.

As a result, marketers themselves can utilize customer data and control messages in a more flexible and proactive manner.

This time, I will test this new feature using exactly the same **“Case Completion Notification Email”** scenario as in the previous article.

## Setup Procedure

## Content Setup

1\. In the previous article, preparation started from the flow side first, but with this feature, configuration begins from the content side.

This time as well, the following email template will be used.

**Subject:**  
**\[Completed\] Your Inquiry Has Been Resolved ({{CaseNumber}})**

**Body:**  
{{LastName}} {{FirstName}},

Thank you very much for your continued support.  
We would like to inform you that the inquiry you submitted has now been resolved.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  
■ **Case Number** : {{CaseNumber}}  
■ **Subject** : {{CaseSubject}}  
■ **Completion Date** : {{ClosedDate}}  
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

If you have any questions or additional inquiries regarding this matter,  
please feel free to reply to this email or contact our support desk.

We will continue striving to provide even better support in the future,  
and we appreciate your continued support.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*SsxmX8h5OG317bXt7e3OZg.png)

2\. After entering the content into the paragraph component, open the **“Data Source”** tab.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*BoMKJmhpiUm4Gm2kBoUgbQ.png)

3\. In the **“Add Content Variable”** section, click the **“Add”** button.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*abFRCSDy7Tf4chIcM6a51A.png)

4\. The following five **“Content Variables”** will be used this time. Create each one from the screen.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*H6wIgCuxvDzYSa9h)

- **Case Number** : `CaseNumber` (**Text Type**)
- **Case Subject** : `CaseSubject` (**Text Type**)
- **Completion Date** : `ClosedDate` (**Text Type**)
- **Last Name** : `LastName` (**Text Type**)
- **First Name** : `FirstName` (**Text Type**)

※ For **Completion Date**, using a **text type** instead of a **date type** allows flexible control over the display format.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*hBGYXCWyCGRmysDx7E69Qg.png)

5\. Once **Content Variables** are configured, they become available as merge fields within the email message.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:863/1*rGw4TWOvcj98GEn3z9focQ.png)

6\. After insertion, it will look like the following.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

_In cases like this, instead of inserting them from the merge field picker, they can also be used directly by writing_ **_“$content.”_** _manually._

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:863/1*c70Up-5YoYtfEdAoaSBcGg.png)

7\. Once the message is complete, **save and publish** it.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*XhP7qCzD_KOQmhpxch8x8Q.png)

## Flow Setup

1\. From the **“Build Your Own”** tab, open **“Event Triggered Flow”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*1ULv9Rv2_J_eZLda)

2\. Open **Flow Builder**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*UHUTyOCLUlUM46UH)

3\. Click the **start element** and select **“Prospect, Lead, Contact or Related Record Change”** from the event library.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*4sqU0BfFDw5ACXhD)

4\. This time as well, select the **“Case”** object, and under it specify **“Contact ID”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*m6LY3CZNAfQf02Wl)

5\. Then configure the **entry conditions** so that the flow starts when the case becomes **“Closed”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Vna68XvG-Ab4zwJ1)

**_Tips:_**_Fields from the triggered record itself are automatically generated as variables and can be directly referenced in subsequent actions. They are displayed as_ **_“{!$Input._**_\*}”\*\*._

_※ In this flow type,_ **_fields from related objects cannot be used._**

6\. Next, since this flow cannot directly access **Contact information**, place a **“Get Records”** element and retrieve the Contact information using the **Contact ID** associated with the Case.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*YxFHVMj29vg5HaqB)

7\. Next, configure the **Marketing Cloud email action**.  
Set the display name and select the email message created earlier.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*DC2ylQsUm6nPyJ9sjKucOw.png)

8\. When selecting an email configured with **“Content Variables”,** an input area for **“Content Variables”** appears at the bottom.

Assign resources to each variable here.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*gF7c5dvkacZFy3fU)

**Description of Each Resource**

① **CaseNumber**  
Use **“Case Number”** from the **Case object**

② **CaseSubject**  
Use **“Subject”** from the **Case object**

③ **ClosedDate**  
**Formula Resource**  
※ Japanese format (**yyyy/MM/dd HH:mm**)

- **API Name:** `fxCompletedDateTimeJp` (arbitrary)
- **Data Type:** `Text`

TEXT(YEAR(DATEVALUE({!$Input.LastModifiedDate} + (9/24)))) & "/" &  
RIGHT("0" & TEXT(MONTH(DATEVALUE({!$Input.LastModifiedDate} + (9/24)))), 2) & "/" &  
RIGHT("0" & TEXT(DAY(DATEVALUE({!$Input.LastModifiedDate} + (9/24)))), 2) & " " &  
RIGHT("0" & TEXT(HOUR(TIMEVALUE({!$Input.LastModifiedDate} + (9/24)))), 2) & ":" &  
RIGHT("0" & TEXT(MINUTE(TIMEVALUE({!$Input.LastModifiedDate} + (9/24)))), 2)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*-WdS9C44HTAU-kh8l2PoLA.png)

④ **FirstName**  
Use **“First Name”** retrieved by **“Get Records”** above

⑤ **LastName**  
Use **“Last Name”** retrieved by **“Get Records”** above

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:860/0*kGIqqu6bddX_CAl5)

9\. Once the **“Content Variables”** assignment is complete, select the **“From Address”** and configure **“Communication Subscription”** if necessary.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*8VGLPE8dwpb_SnAb)

10\. Finally, **save and activate** the flow.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*zPBF8vuVAAixJCFP)

## Complete the Case and Send the Email

1\. After activating the flow, change the case status to **“Closed”.  
**As shown below, the flow will be triggered.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:795/0*DdVqKyQvgqGr6qho)

2\. The email was actually sent successfully, and I was able to confirm that the content was personalized correctly. **Success!!**

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:868/1*cY1yps-zQ8rHoxJczBfjgA.png)

## Conclusion

My personal impression is that the implementation effort itself does not feel dramatically different compared to using **Apex**. However, for marketers who feel resistance toward Apex, being able to build personalized emails **without code development** is a very significant advantage.

Also, this mechanism can be used across various flow types, but the types of resources available and their behavior have unique characteristics depending on the flow. Therefore, I recommend testing thoroughly while confirming which resources and send conditions are actually available.

title: SFMC Tips #291 : Marketing Cloud Next: Shared IP Addresses and Dedicated IP Addresses
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--5237675268e2---------------------------------------)

4 min read

May 12, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*-zz990mxyUoN2YHt)

Photo by [Tide\_trasher\_x](https://unsplash.com/@tide_trasher_x?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

In the [Spring ’26 new feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-spring-26-release-highlights-24c0c804b0cb) for Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions, the long-awaited **“Dedicated IP Address”** feature was introduced. Until now, email sending had only been performed through **“Shared IP Addresses”.**

You can check the currently used IP address type and which IP pool you belong to from the following screen.

- **Settings ＞ Unified Messaging ＞ Email ＞ Settings ＞ Sending IP Addresses**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Egpn4c8Ql2BrVIaI)

## How It Works

Dedicated IP addresses in Marketing Cloud Next are **not something that needs to be purchased separately from Salesforce as an additional option.**

When the monthly sending volume from Marketing Cloud Next exceeds a certain threshold (**approximately 5 million emails per month as a guideline**), the system automatically detects it and begins the migration process to **Dedicated IP Addresses**.

After that, when the sending volume reaches around **10 million emails per month**, a **second Dedicated IP Address** is assigned. As the volume continues to increase, additional IPs are assigned sequentially.

In addition, if the sending volume continues to remain below the threshold for a certain period of time, the assigned Dedicated IP Addresses are sequentially reclaimed, and eventually sending is returned to **Shared IP Addresses**.

For those who are familiar with Marketing Cloud Engagement, this may feel like a unique approach.

There are also the following mechanisms.

## About IP Warm-Up

When migration to Dedicated IPs begins, **IP warm-up is automatically performed by Salesforce.**

The warm-up period is carried out over approximately **0 to 35 days** after the migration starts.

In addition, if sending volume exceeds the limit during the warm-up period, the excess portion continues to be sent from Shared IPs, so users do not need to finely control sending volume themselves.

As a result, the traditional **“manual IP warm-up process” basically becomes unnecessary.** However, this only applies to **“IP reputation”.** From the perspective of **domain reputation (domain warm-up),** it is still necessary to maintain a gradual sending plan and stable sending quality.

## About Dedicated IP Address Reclamation

If the average sending volume over the most recent **45 days** falls below the threshold (**approximately 5 million emails per month as a guideline**), the assigned Dedicated IP Addresses are sequentially reclaimed.

Furthermore, if only the final **1 IP** remains and sending below **5 million emails per month** continues for **90 days**, it is automatically returned to Shared IP Addresses.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

The reclaimed Dedicated IP Addresses are reused after a certain cooldown period.

## Introduction of the Gray IP Address Pool

Among Shared IP users, accounts with high bounce rates (**approximately 5% as a guideline**) are temporarily moved to a dedicated IP pool called the **“Gray Pool”.**

This is an area separated from the normal Shared IP pool and Dedicated IP pool, and emails are sent from this pool until the bounce rate improves.

This mechanism helps maintain the overall health of both the Shared IP pool and Dedicated IP pool.

## Outside the Scope of SLA (Service Level Agreement)

Please note that the figures introduced here are only **“guidelines”.**

These are considered **outside the scope of the SLA (Service Level Agreement),** so even if there are cases such as:

> _“Sending volume exceeds 5 million emails per month, but migration to Dedicated IPs does not happen immediately.”_

it is necessary to rely on Salesforce’s control and judgment.

What did you think?

For those who have been using Marketing Cloud Engagement for a long time, this may appear to be a very interesting mechanism.

The fact that **“IP warm-up may no longer be necessary”** is extremely attractive, but the importance of **sending quality itself** remains unchanged.

In particular, providers such as **Google** are increasingly emphasizing **“domain reputation”** in recent years.

Therefore, the following basic practices for maintaining sending quality continue to be extremely important regardless of whether you use Shared IPs or Dedicated IPs:

- **Do not send to invalid email addresses**
- **Prioritize sending to high-quality customers who are likely to open and click**
- **Minimize unsubscribes and complaints**

That is all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

## Nobuyuki Watanabe

[https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/) (+Follow me)

title: SFMC Tips #292 : Marketing Cloud Next: CRM Record Query Flow
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--8bfb3e7c3d74---------------------------------------)

6 min read

May 13, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*rFHSl7XTuUu98Lwl)

Photo by [Evgeni Tcherkasski](https://unsplash.com/@evgenit?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions [Summer ’26 new feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-summer-26-release-highlights-04f6c5abdee6) has renamed the entry point of the traditional “Segment Triggered Flow” to **“Audience Flow”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*8C0N4LH9Chm4QX1p)

Within this **Audience Flow**, when creating a flow, users can now select various data sources from the **Start** element depending on the use case, in addition to the traditional **Segment Flow**, including:

- **CRM Record Query Flow (Record)**
- **List Triggered Flow (List)**
- **Campaign Member Flow (Campaign)**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*h5PQczYXXMtCwtBz)

As a result, audience-based flows have now been unified under the **Audience Flow** category, making flow creation and management simpler and easier to understand.

_Regarding the_ **_Campaign Flow_** _used for sending messages to_ **_campaign members_**_, it was previously possible to segment and send to campaign members through campaign flows. However, with this update, the functionality has become more intuitive and can now be used with a single click._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*BcUQ0BM9-T3ION1K)

In this article, I would like to focus specifically on the particularly noteworthy **CRM Record Query Flow (Record)** and explain it in detail.

## What is a CRM Record Query Flow?

The **CRM Record Query Flow**, newly introduced in the Summer ’26 release, is a new flow type that directly sets conditions on CRM records in the “Start” element and retrieves the target audience from CRM records **in real time immediately before execution** for sending.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*QD9W9MnOTR8kRoX4)

In this CRM Record Query Flow, instead of sending based on **“Unified Individuals”** like traditional segment-based delivery, it becomes possible to perform scheduled sends using the **latest records stored on the CRM side** as the data source.

For search conditions, in addition to **AND** and **OR**, **custom logic** can also be used, making it possible to define complex conditions such as:

**“(1 AND 2 AND 3) OR 4”**

_Note: Since the search conditions are evaluated for the first time immediately before the flow starts, the send count and retrieved audience cannot be confirmed in advance. Therefore,_ **_it is recommended to initially configure only a “Wait” element and perform a dry run to verify the actual send count before enabling the flow_**_._

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:760/0*FcapTN_bn1DqO3E-.png)

## How Data Graphs Are Handled

What many people are probably wondering here is how **“Data Graphs”** used in content and flows are handled.

In this CRM Record Query Flow, even if merge fields derived from **Data Graphs** are included in the email content, **those values are not referenced.**

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

More precisely, **Data Graph values are not retrieved, and the configured “default values” are displayed instead.** Even if the audience actually has accessible values in the Data Graph, the **“default values”** will still be displayed.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*GRfw_ngDQyKLuUIl)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Fs0qmyM9uKTlQga6)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*fqOtCRBgnFVPxp90)

On the other hand, in the **“Decision”** element within the flow, **condition evaluation using Data Graph values is possible.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Xqzi_e6Hqc150R5X)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*jvutIACw-xRSMpzF)

In other words:

- **Data Graphs are not used for content merge fields**
- **However, Data Graphs can be used for flow conditional branching**

This results in somewhat unusual behavior.

## Use “Content Variables” for Personalization

However, if you absolutely need to personalize content, please use personalization through **“Content Variables”.**

With this new feature released in Summer ’26, it becomes possible to **pass variables directly from the flow to the email message.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*jT3e8S0taKGXJw1A)

By the way, the **“Attribute” resource** of the Flow Profile used below receives the **Individual values from the Data Graph.** For any fields you want to use in the flow, make sure they are enabled beforehand in the Data Graph configuration.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*h8Kv2WiAnzboX_H3)

As shown below, the name is inserted using the **“Content Variable”** feature.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*IuB_K4dxMUMtqyYO)

### Considerations

In this example, the Flow Profile attribute resource was used, but for example, if the contact record was just newly created and Data Graph data has not yet been generated, **missing Data Graph data may cause an internal error during sending**, resulting in retry status. If the issue is not resolved during the retry period, it will ultimately become a send error.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*PJtaqroAAbipAjpp)

Conversely, if the issue is resolved during the retry period, the send will occur at the next retry timing. Therefore, if Data Graphs are updated during late-night hours, **emails may also be sent late at night**, so please be careful.

**_Tips:_** _If retries similar to congestion are occurring, consider that the send may occur during the first retry after the issue is resolved._

## Latest CRM Records Can Be Used Immediately

The **biggest feature** of this CRM Record Query Flow is that **there is no need to wait for synchronization to Data 360.**

For example, even a contact record created just **one minute ago** can immediately be used for delivery execution. The email address used at this time is the contact’s **standard email field.**

This mechanism is a **very impactful sending method** that significantly changes the traditional concept of Marketing Cloud Next, where delivery was based on data ingested into Data 360 and unified as a **Unified Individual.**

On the other hand, there are also important considerations.

Earlier, I explained that **Data Graphs can be used in the “Decision” element**, but the latest CRM records are **not instantly reflected in the Data Graph.**

In other words:

- **The delivery target itself can use the latest CRM records**
- **However, the Data Graph information may not yet be updated**

This situation can occur.

Therefore, with this CRM Record Query Flow, **it is important to avoid excessive personalization as much as possible and use it mainly for simple bulk delivery purposes.**

↑ ↑ ↑

**This is my personal opinion.**

What did you think?

With the traditional **Marketing List Email** functionality, customers extracted through list views were synchronized to Marketing Cloud Next segments for use, but it felt heavily dependent on the **Data Cloud** and **Marketing Cloud Next** environment.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:863/0*3Nz4JZueMOTJ28o4)

On the other hand, by using this **CRM Record Query** feature, **CRM records can be searched directly**, enabling more flexible audience creation.

That’s all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

## Nobuyuki Watanabe

[https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/) (+Follow me)

title: SFMC Tips #293 : Marketing Cloud Next: Retrieving a Specific Record from a Data Graph in Marketing…
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--e03c87cea117---------------------------------------)

5 min read

May 14, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*tOFdFnzwT46kDNm-)

Photo by [Jon Tyson](https://unsplash.com/@jontyson?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

With the [Summer ’26 new feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-summer-26-release-highlights-04f6c5abdee6) for **Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions**, source data such as **Data Graphs** can now be narrowed down in advance using **“sorting”** and **“search conditions (filtering)”** within the Flow **“Resource Manager”** and **“Resource Selection Fields”.**

This makes it possible to retrieve and utilize **“one specific record that you want to use in the flow”** from sources that contain multiple records, such as Data Graphs.

For example:

- **“Most recently purchased product”**
- **“Latest login history”**
- **“Highest-value order information”**

It is now possible to select and handle **“one representative record”** from array data, which improves the flexibility of personalization and conditional branching within flows.

### **Important**

This mechanism is only available in the following flow types:

- **Segment Triggered Flow**
- **Activation Triggered Flow**

## Previous Limitation

For example, consider the case where a Data Graph is used in a **“Decision”** element.

With the previous mechanism, related attributes in the Data Graph were treated as **arrays (multiple records).**

Therefore, even if multiple purchase histories existed in **“Sales Order Product Engagement”,** the only thing that could be evaluated was:

**_“Whether at least one record among multiple records matches the condition.”_**

In other words, it was not possible to perform detailed condition evaluations targeting **“one specific record”,** such as:

- **“I only want to look at the latest purchase history”**
- **“I want to branch based on the newest order record”**
- **“I only want to target the highest-value order”**

When directly selecting the Data Graph, it looked like the following.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*FcmtXdfy8nOJBelZ)

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*QWoZz7xAmRmTwD2a)

So, with this new feature, it has now become possible to:

**_“Extract one representative record from multiple records for use within the flow.”_**

In fact, this concept itself had already been introduced earlier on the **Content Builder** side.

For example, when creating a content **“Expression”** or configuring personalization fields, you have probably already configured:

- **Sorting**
- **Search Conditions (Filtering)**

The same mechanism has finally been introduced into flows as well.

## Configuration Steps

### 1. Open Resource Manager and click “New Resource”.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*65uSMRepduUkWBoM)

### 2. As a resource type, a new option called “Collection Filter Criteria” has been added.

In Salesforce Help, this is introduced under the name **“Selected Record”.**

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:869/0*h8zLuAKxJc5sOdNG)

### 3. Here, you can select “related attributes in a Data Graph” as the data source.

Needless to say, direct attributes are unrelated to today’s topic.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:869/0*POU-as3mYAsplW6u)

### 4. This time, since we want to retrieve the “most recently purchased product”

(API Name: **latestPurchasedProductName**), select **“Sales Order Product Engagement”.**

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

Select **“Entire Resource”** and complete the setup.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:873/0*xC7xk9rAPsqExKZs)

### 5. After selecting the DMO, the following areas will appear at the bottom of the screen:

- **Top section: Sort Conditions — required**
- **Bottom section: Filter Conditions — optional**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:866/0*toyBEJWq3lzO_cjE)

### 6. The important points here are as follows.

### Important Notes for “Sort Conditions”

- You can only select fields from the **exact same DMO** as the data source.  
  Although the UI allows you to select fields from other DMOs, doing so will actually result in an error.

### Important Notes for “Filter Conditions”

- You can use fields from **all DMOs that were traversed while selecting the data source.  
  **However, fields from DMOs that were not traversed will result in errors.

Please configure the settings with this in mind.

### 7. This time, we simply configure “Record Created Date” in descending order.

In other words, the configuration is:

**_“Retrieve the newest purchase history as the representative record”_**

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:865/0*ApykmiVG73pzLzcE)

### 8. This creates “latestPurchasedProductName” as a “Collection Filter Criteria” resource generated from Data Graph data.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*0vLPEThChRtkXBCz)

### 9. This time, we will use this resource in a “Decision” element.

Other use cases, such as using it in an **“Assignment”** element, are also possible.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*vEMU8vXbwdGmwdAX)

### 10. From the one representative record retrieved in “latestPurchasedProductName”, select the “Product” field.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*gurXoramksOCBudS)

### 11. Then specify the product name that should match the “most recently purchased product”.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*xEHoCS9WTbBlJPv9)

### 12. This now makes it possible to branch only when:

**_“The latest purchased product is ○○.”_**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*dWgaz5DGQRzswQUx)

What did you think?

I believe this is one of those **“small but extremely useful”** types of features.

It is not a flashy feature with immediate impact, but when you think:

- **“Can’t I perform this kind of conditional evaluation?”**
- **“I only want to use the latest record”.**
- **“I want to select only one record from multiple records”.**

it is important to remember:

**_“Oh right, there was such a mechanism in Resource Manager 🤔”_**

Conversely, if you never become aware of the feature called **“Collection Filter Criteria (Selected Record)”,** it may very well become a feature you never use for your entire life.

Especially if you want to implement **advanced personalization** or **conditional branching using Data Graphs**, I believe this will become a very important mechanism going forward.

That is all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

markdown:

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

What did you think?

I felt this was a very useful improvement for addressing audit-related questions that are also commonly seen in Marketing Cloud Engagement, such as:

> _“Who was the last person that edited this automation or journey?”_

With this new capability in Marketing Cloud Next, it is now much easier to review edit history and trace changes safely.

In addition, even after making major temporary changes for debugging purposes, it is now **very easy to revert back to the production configuration**.

Previously, many organizations managed this by creating separate flows or separate versions, so this should also help **reduce operational overhead**.

On the other hand, as the number of save histories increases, it may become difficult to identify which version was the official production version.

For that reason, it may also become important operationally to keep a simple memo of the **“final approved save date and time”.**

That’s all for this time.

title: SFMC Tips #295 : Marketing Cloud Next: Create Custom Web Tracking Consent Banners
markdown:
## Creating the Banner

1\. From the **Content** tab, select Create New Content, and choose the newly added **Consent Banner** option introduced in Summer ’26.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:805/0*Ctsk8ZENUwTms7Gv)

2\. When the editor screen opens, a banner preview is displayed in a website mockup format. By default, the layout is configured to appear at the bottom of the screen.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*iJCmMlOyIOxLsiPB)

3\. The banner can be flexibly configured, including its display position and size.

With this update, in addition to the traditional top and bottom placement options, **center placement on the screen** is now also supported.

Additionally, **mobile display optimization** is automatically applied.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*dH0Dhr-d9IhTzO88)

4\. The banner can also reflect the **brand** **colors** and **styles** configured in your organization.

As a result, it can be displayed with a consistent look and feel that matches the overall website design.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*vL8PMYAwpYAQu68G)

5\. One of the most significant improvements in this update is that button text can now be freely customized.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

Previously, fixed English labels such as “**Accept**” and “**Reject**” were always displayed, but now any text can be configured.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*JCqmIkPb_wgPzulo)

6\. After completing all settings, save and then publish the banner.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*FLoxMdHv7iSJB4lO)

## Configuring the Banner

1\. From Settings, open Marketing Features ＞ Web Tracking ＞ **Landing Page Tracking**, and select **Select Banner**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*fVNb0-hzYr3HAUCX)

2\. Select the custom banner created earlier and save the settings.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*nfbKAD-xYAtbOiJ2)

3\. Once the settings are saved, the association is complete. However, at this stage, **it is still not reflected on the landing page**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*myKVm_fzbWQQn6IV)

4\. To actually apply the banner to the landing page, search for **All Sites** in Settings, and open the **Marketing Landing Page Builder**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*1vbuvuhg4eRSl-BV)

5\. Once the Builder screen opens, no additional editing is required. Simply click **Publish** as is.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*C1VGr9fglx6MLxxE)

By the way, if this is your first time configuring a consent banner, you must first complete the following initial setup steps in advance.

6\. After waiting for the publishing process to complete, the banner will appear on the landing page. The setup is now complete.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*IcM71mQAqINALqDy)

## Conclusion

Until now, consent banner buttons could only display the fixed English labels “**Accept**” and “**Reject**”, which created limitations in terms of expression and localization.

However, with the **Summer ’26 new feature release**, flexibility required for real-world operations has been greatly improved, including:

- **Free customization of button text**
- **Design adjustments**
- **Brand integration**
- **Support for center placement**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*DktbPA_oY2ihiumK)

Example of the previous standard consent banner

At first glance, this may appear to be a small update. However, adding flexibility to areas that previously could not be modified is actually a very significant improvement.

Especially in countries and regions where English is not the primary language, this customization will likely become almost essential in order to obtain consent from users in a more natural and localized way.

Be sure to review the setup process early.

That’s all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

title: SFMC Tips #296 : Marketing Cloud Next: Lookup Data Graph Data Provider
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--0054d3fcdd83---------------------------------------)

5 min read

May 17, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*5VSuLOWYHVvZ3gZS)

Photo by [Kiki Siepel](https://unsplash.com/@studiokiek?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

In the [Spring ’26 new feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-spring-26-release-highlights-24c0c804b0cb) for Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions, a **Lookup Data Graph** became available, allowing existing profile data graphs to be enhanced by referencing **“data that is not directly tied to people”** such as product catalogs.

- With this feature, you can add up to **five Lookup Data Graph Data Providers** to a single email message.
- When using a lookup data graph as a data source, you specify a **Primary Key (PK)** and dynamically retrieve data related to the message recipient using that value.

## Why Use a “Lookup” Structure?

Why was this kind of **lookup-based** mechanism designed in the first place?

For example, sales data and purchase history are **data tied to individuals**, in the sense of “who purchased what”.

Therefore, it does not feel unnatural to associate them with a **profile data graph** centered around Unified Individual.

On the other hand, a product catalog itself is not originally data tied to a “person”.

In other words, if massive and independent master data such as:

- Product catalogs
- Inventory information
- Price masters
- Campaign information

were all directly nested and stored inside the profile data graph, there is a concern that the data graph would become bloated and architecturally unhealthy.

In particular, data such as product catalogs are not constantly used in every scenario.

There are many cases where:

> _“It is sufficient to retrieve only the necessary data at the necessary timing.”_

For that reason, it is likely that Lookup Data Graph Data Providers were prepared as a mechanism to keep such data separated from the profile data graph and dynamically reference it only when needed.

In addition, data such as:

- Inventory quantity
- Pricing
- Campaign information

often has a high update frequency and requires real-time characteristics.

If such data were included directly in a Unified Individual–based data graph, there is a possibility that the overall update load and recalculation cost of the data graph would become significantly larger.

Therefore, in many cases, it may be more appropriate to separate highly volatile master data from the profile data graph and reference it only when necessary.

Incidentally, Salesforce Personalization is also designed in a similar way, where data is managed separately as:

- [Salesforce Personalization Profile Data Graph](https://agentforce-marketing-9cf347fa7db7.herokuapp.com/workshops/salesforce-personalization/profile-data-graphs.html)
- [Salesforce Personalization Item Data Graph](https://agentforce-marketing-9cf347fa7db7.herokuapp.com/workshops/salesforce-personalization/item-data-graphs.html)

## Configuration Method

For example, let us assume that under the current specification, Sales Order Product Engagement stores the “product number” of a sold product, but does not store the “product name”.

In this case, you can use the “product number” held by Sales Order Product Engagement as the **Primary Key (PK)** and reference the “product number” on the Goods Product data graph side to retrieve the “product name” stored in the Goods Product data graph.

### 1. Add a Data Source

First, open the content, go to the “Data Sources” tab, and click “Add”.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*LKLt9qLMXvTMlL-r)

### 2. Select Lookup Data Graph Data Provider

Before deciding the Name (API Name), open **Lookup Data Graph Data Provider**.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

_It is recommended to enter the Name (API Name) immediately before saving because it may get cleared during the setup process._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*mFzZ0krZ8T2CFrHE)

### 3. Select the Lookup Target Data Graph

In Lookup Data Graph, open the product data graph called “Products”.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*sksfnKLQdQi-tmTG)

### 4. Configure the Primary Key

For the Primary Key, select a **merge field**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*_ZKg9OmJFpPdHQW_)

Since the Product field in Sales Order Product Engagement represents the product ID, select that field.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*xuv1zL9qgCI9I98E)

### 5. Configure the Name (API Name) and Save

Once the Primary Key has been entered, input the Name (API Name) and save the configuration.

_It is recommended to use a Name (API Name) that is easy to understand later. Using the data graph name itself is probably a good choice._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Dnn_603DQV2hCtG0)

## How to Use

Once the setup is complete, a **second data graph** becomes available in the merge fields.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*IgmWfbDM2sm_9z3l)

This makes it possible to dynamically display information in email messages that is not stored within the profile data graph alone, such as:

- Product names
- Product descriptions
- Product images
- Latest pricing

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Q0kCYP9XJYlWNMbA)

When previewed, it appears as shown below.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*QTfns3z-MGptrh_N)

What did you think?

It may be easier to understand Lookup Data Graphs if you think of them as a mechanism for loosely coupling **“behavioral data tied to individuals”** with **“independent master data”.**

However, I do think the difficulty of reaching this level of configuration is somewhat high.

In environments already implementing Salesforce Personalization and similar solutions, there may be cases where this can be utilized as-is, but personally, I also have some doubts about whether it is necessary to forcefully build out this configuration from scratch.

If the same requirement can be implemented more simply, prioritizing the simpler approach may be more realistic.

Rather than something that must be used aggressively, I think it is better viewed as:

> _“Use it when the opportunity naturally arises.”_

That is all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

## Nobuyuki Watanabe

[https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/) (+Follow me)

title: SFMC Tips #297 : Marketing Cloud Next: How to Use the “Exit from a Flow” Element
markdown:
What did you think?

As a use case example, you could prepare a separate flow using something like an Event Triggered Flow, Data Cloud Triggered Flow, or On-Demand Flow, and execute this **“Exit from a Flow”** action at the moment a customer makes a purchase. This would allow another running Segment Flow to be terminated in an almost near real-time manner.

This could potentially be built much more simply compared to implementing a custom API-based solution.

In particular, for exit criteria and decision logic using data graphs, some fields may only be updated approximately once per day depending on the data source. In scenarios where you want to **immediately exclude someone from a flow**, this mechanism may work very effectively.

It can also be used for cases such as segmenting customers who encountered some kind of issue or exception condition and forcibly excluding them from the Segment Flow side.

Rather than viewing it as just a simple stop control mechanism, I personally felt this is a very interesting feature when viewed as a **control mechanism for supplementing real-time behavior**.

That’s all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

## Nobuyuki Watanabe

title: SFMC Tips #298 : Marketing Cloud Next: Creating a Signup Form (Simplified Version)
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--43e797302478---------------------------------------)

7 min read

May 19, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*fHMPV_nONLjbOl7O)

Photo by [kiryl](https://unsplash.com/@kshar2?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

In the [**Summer ’26** feature release](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-summer-26-release-highlights-04f6c5abdee6) for **Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions**, several improvements were added to the **campaign flow creation experience** and **flow template functionality**.

This article explains the following points:

- ① The default start tab changed from **“Quick Start”** to **“Build Your Own”**
- ② Direct access to “flow templates” is now available from **“Build Your Own”**
- ③ Easy setup is now possible with the new **“Signup Form”** flow template

## ① The Default Start Tab Changed from “Quick Start” to “Build Your Own”

When creating a flow from a campaign record, the default entry point has changed from the previously displayed **“Quick Start”** tab to the **“Build Your Own”** tab.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:855/0*8Beo2dzMW5bD4lvL)

From this **“Build Your Own”** tab, it is now possible to configure campaign flow starting settings and browse flow templates, making it easier to build flows more flexibly.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*duyF3WMYRSzPqA5S)

Of course, it is still possible to use the **“Quick Start”** tab to quickly launch campaigns based on preconfigured flows, just like before.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*_ENvwuAbLl6Oq5JO)

## ② Direct Access to Flow Templates from “Build Your Own”

With this improvement, marketers can now directly access the **Flow Template Library** from the **“Build Your Own”** tab.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*7m8ADVzJ-mKoa8mw)

Flow templates already include flow elements for common marketing use cases, reducing the effort required to design flows from scratch and enabling smoother implementation.

As of **Summer ’26**, the following four flow templates are available.

## Flow Templates

## 1. Event Email Automation Template

This template is designed to encourage customers to attend webinars, conferences, product launches, and other events.

A series of event communications, such as registration confirmations, reminders, and post-event follow-ups, are preconfigured.

Included content:

- Email 1: Event One Week Reminder
- Email 2: Event Registration Confirmation
- Email 3: Event One Day Reminder
- Email 4: Event Attended Follow-Up
- Email 5: Event Didn’t Attend Follow-Up

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*BYV10dD1skbhEDgF)

## 2. Lead Capture Follow-Up Email Automation Template

This template is designed for follow-up communications that help convert leads into customers.

Emails such as welcome messages, brand introductions, and product/service highlights are preconfigured.

Included content:

- Email 1: Lead Capture Welcome Email
- Email 2: Lead Capture Product Highlights
- Email 3: Lead Capture Brand Value Proposition

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:490/0*lu_HyxP3GfSi9Vvl.png)

## 3. Upsert a Record for a Person Template

If a matching **Contact** or **Lead** record exists, this template updates the person’s record.  
If no matching record exists, it creates or updates a **Prospect** record.

This flow helps maintain **data consistency** across related records.

_\*Because this template is intended for use as a subflow, it is created as an_ **_Autolaunched Flow (No Trigger)_**_._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*pQHkO7BreYNe_VFX)

This template is explained in detail in the following article.

## 4. Newly Added: “Signup Form” Template

This template uses signup forms to generate leads and collect consent for any messaging channel.

_\*This template is explained in the next section._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*AWDwF5ul6_3ySFVB)

## Deprecation of Flow Templates

Previously, there were six marketing-related flow templates available, but currently only the following four remain.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*1phww4l7tz3q2Kzu)

**Marketing Flow Template Library After Summer ’26**

The following three templates may have been removed in **Summer ’26**.  
(_This will be confirmed when the official production release becomes available._)

- Birthday Promotional Email Automation Template
- Nurture Campaign Email Automation Template
- Reengagement Email Automation Template

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*rpZuhLVHlpd1HNt0)

**Marketing Flow Template Library Before Summer ’26**

## ③ Easy Setup with the New “Signup Form” Flow Template

Previously, when using **“Signup Form”** from **Quick Start**,

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*3hOvkWiph6lhUSTu)

it was necessary to configure each step individually, such as:

1. Forms
2. Flows
3. Landing Pages

With the new ability to start directly from a flow template, these configuration tasks can now be completed together in a unified setup process, greatly reducing the complexity that was often difficult for beginners.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

As a result, lead generation campaigns can now be built much more simply and smoothly.

## Setup Procedure

## 1. Select the Flow Template

Select **“Signup Form”** from the Flow Template Library.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*BW8tTJc4cTLT7_Nd)

Click **“Next”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*bYkJG3Fq4Ezf071o)

## 2. Configure the Flow Name

Enter the flow name.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*oeB49QpikXwVSXXm)

## 3. Configure the Processing Object and Communication Subscription

Configure the target object and communication subscription settings.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*qpKWLZeSfmjI2IX4)

## 4. Configure Branding

Configure the branding settings.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*derJ8WXoTh_LzLbf)

_In some cases, branding settings may not be reflected on the landing page side.  
If this occurs, configure branding separately on the landing page afterward._

## 5. Configure the Form Content

Configure the form field labels.

Also configure the header, footer, button text, and related settings.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*BLLB0HkR7EVeeyKL)

**_Important:_** _Some fields are treated as required and cannot be removed._ ⚠️

## 6. Decide Whether to Use a Landing Page

A landing page is not required for signup forms.

Choose whether to add a landing page, then click **“Create”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*VGv-B1wwGQ4FDyww)

## 7. Change the URL Alias

It is strongly recommended to change the URL alias beforehand.

_An important point to note is that_ **_once the landing page is published even once, the URL alias can no longer be changed afterward._** ⚠️

Click the action button on the landing page.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*ww9djxI_92wUIEtt)

Click **“Edit URL Details”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*mgvBncy2W_cDAJae)

Rewrite the URL alias and save it.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*o7oz65mO0qzIpb0i)

## 8. Publish the Form

The item displayed under the **Start** section at the top represents the form settings.

Click **“Edit”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*GQSQPOmgYZMz1Fqg)

The form screen opens. Review the content, and if there are no issues, publish it as-is.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*hxX270g0o22T0qET)

_It may take several minutes for publishing to complete.  
The status may remain as_ **_“Draft”_** _temporarily, but this is normal.  
Once publishing is complete, a notification email will be sent._

## 9. Verify the Flow

Next, open the flow.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*JQzYAIIT0d1lzcBm)

When the form was published earlier, the flow was automatically activated.

After confirming that everything is configured correctly, close the flow.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:825/0*HFx71acEdGmwo2VQ)

**_Note:_** _In the default configuration, the_ **_Email_** _field may not be mapped in the_ **_Create Records_** _element.  
If the mapping is missing, create a new version and add the Email mapping manually._ ⚠️

## 10. Publish the Landing Page

Finally, publish the landing page.

This process also takes several minutes to complete.  
Once finished, a notification email will be sent.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*Sl3T-eHbgP8nFAt5)

## 11. Retrieve the Landing Page URL

The setup is now complete.

To retrieve the landing page URL, click **“View Record”.**

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*tpiOLG2OPcPrBqXA)

Open the **“URL”** tab to confirm the landing page URL.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*H8LND13LG11XTBmu)

How was it?

To be honest, regarding this new **“Signup Form”** mechanism, I personally felt that the previous method provided a stronger sense of being able to carefully review and configure each screen step by step.

Additionally, at the current stage, there are still many rough edges (such as Email not being mapped, or fields incorrectly treated as required and impossible to remove). In practice, it feels like some manual adjustments and edits are still necessary to make everything behave as expected. Hopefully these issues will be improved in future minor updates.

On the other hand, the previous setup process was quite complicated for beginners, and there were probably many cases where users became confused midway through the configuration process.

This new flow may also feel confusing at first, but once users become familiar with it, it may actually become a very efficient way to launch campaigns.

I plan to continue testing it in real use cases and evaluate its usability and operational behavior over time.

For reference, the setup flow of the previous **“Signup Form”** method is explained in the following article.

That’s all for this time.

Stay tuned for more Salesforce Marketing Cloud tips! 😎

## Nobuyuki Watanabe

[https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nobuyuki-watanabe/) (+Follow me)

title: SFMC Tips #301 : Marketing Cloud Next: A Free Developer Edition Available to Everyone
markdown:
[

![Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:40:40/1*zLRnWqPVgEgwGDwBBiwV3w.png)



](/@marketingcloudtips?source=post_page---byline--9afcac83f318---------------------------------------)

6 min read

6 days ago

2

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*j4mVCgD-JJ4nEu-O)

Photo by [Camp Paradise](https://unsplash.com/@camp_paradise?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

As of May 2026, when it came to a free development environment for Marketing Cloud Next Growth & Advanced Editions, the practical option was [SDO (Simple Demo Org), which is provided to Salesforce partners](/@marketingcloudtips/marketing-cloud-next-basic-setup-procedure-for-the-demo-environment-be441f7c37d8).

As a result, for many people, Marketing Cloud Next remained in a situation of “I’m interested in it, but I don’t have an environment where I can actually use it”.

However, a **Marketing Cloud Next Developer Edition has finally appeared in Trailhead Playground**, **which is available to everyone**.

In terms of functionality, it is equivalent to the Marketing Cloud Next Advanced Edition (higher edition) environment, meaning that a “Marketing Cloud Developer Edition available to everyone” has finally been provided — something that was never achieved with Marketing Cloud Engagement.

It can currently be accessed from the following Trailhead.

## Things to Note When Using It

At this time, there are several limitations.

- **Email sending is not available**.
- **The usage period is limited to 3 days per instance**.

Considering the nature of the Trailhead Playground environment, these restrictions are understandable to some extent.

That said, I believe the significance of a publicly available environment where Marketing Cloud Next can actually be used is enormous.

## A Major Step Forward

Until now, there were likely many people who could not move forward with learning or testing because:

✅ “There is no environment.”  
✅ “I cannot test the features.”  
✅ “I do not know where to start.”

With this Developer Edition, anyone can now:

- Freely navigate the UI
- Explore and verify features firsthand
- Gain practical experience with Marketing Cloud Next

For those who have wanted to learn Marketing Cloud Next, this is truly an excellent opportunity.

I highly encourage you to take this opportunity to start exploring Marketing Cloud Next.

I would also like to personally test how far I can go with the Trailhead Playground, which features are available, and which features have limitations.

_For reference, I have already confirmed that Data Graphs work without any issues. Once Summer ’26 is reflected, it should also become possible to test features such as AMPscript._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:830/0*AScrysfVsvCMW_lT)

## Learning Resources You Can Use

The resources available for learning Marketing Cloud Next have expanded significantly over the past few months.

## Agentforce Marketing Workshop

Recently, Salesforce’s official Agentforce Marketing Workshop site became generally available (GA).

Not only has the design been refreshed, but the amount of content has also increased substantially, with a large number of learning resources related to Marketing Cloud Next, Data Cloud, and Agentforce now available.

This is especially recommended for those learning Marketing Cloud Next, Salesforce Personalization, and Data Cloud.

## The Agentic Marketer (TAM)

This is the most active MCN community among the overseas communities officially recognized by Salesforce.

The latest information and practical know-how are shared there, so I highly recommend joining the Slack community as well.

## Arthur Backouche’s Blog

A large amount of deep technical information related to Marketing Cloud Next and Data Cloud has been published there.

[https://arthurbackouche.com/docs/marketing-cloud-next/](https://arthurbackouche.com/docs/marketing-cloud-next/)

There are also many practical use cases and validation articles, making it an extremely valuable resource.

## Conclusion

The Marketing Cloud Next ecosystem is currently growing rapidly.

## Get Nobuyuki Watanabe @marketingcloudtips’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Remember me for faster sign in

Learning content and community resources continue to expand, and access to development environments has become significantly easier than before.

And with the arrival of the Trailhead Playground Developer Edition, it is fair to say that the barrier to getting started with Marketing Cloud Next is now at its lowest level ever.

I encourage you to take advantage of these resources, continue your learning journey, and experience the possibilities of Marketing Cloud Next for yourself.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/1*nCWKZQ3HaiwlCYky6Rzxog.png)

⚠️ The issue below was already resolved by Salesforce on June 3, 2026. However, I will leave this information here for a few more days for reference and record-keeping purposes.

## Workaround: Send an Email Campaign (Step 3)

Currently, **Send an Email Campaign (Step 3)** in Trailhead cannot be completed by following the instructions provided in Trailhead as-is.

Salesforce will likely fix this issue in the near future. 🤔 However, since I have received many inquiries about this problem, I would like to share a way to complete the Challenge at this time.

The reason for the issue is that the flow’s **Progress Status** must become **Completed**, but in the free Trailhead Playground environment, email sending is not allowed and no authenticated domain is configured. As a result, a flow containing an email cannot be activated.

You can work around this issue using the following steps.

1\. You should already have created a flow similar to the following.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*tQr7YIQFl61mUXeC)

2\. At this point, the Progress Status is **Draft**. The Challenge completion requirement is for this status to become **Completed**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*-eERQeVDjuXEvmEt)

3\. When you try to activate the flow, you will likely see an error “We can’t find an active campaign.” similar to the following.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*JPhu9JymO1b-zxLM)

4\. Leave the flow you are currently creating in **Draft** status and create a new version.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*zCaO_LDFj78pybsg)

5\. After Version 2 is created, delete the Send Email Message element.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*gjVRdlW48su_ouWm)

6\. Instead, add a **Wait for Amount of Time** element.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*OU3eH7e7bOlGd6_A)

7\. Set the wait time to **1 minute**.

_If you set a longer wait time, you will not be able to complete the Challenge until that amount of time has passed. Therefore, I recommend setting it to 1 minute._

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*RcYQZFk36B1oUMfw)

8\. Save the flow and activate it. With this configuration, the error will not occur.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*LEKjx-KHrxgasynK)

9\. Immediately after activation, the Progress Status will become **Activated**. In this state, the Challenge still cannot be completed. The status must be **Completed**.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*gxg8LhuFiE0lHSAN)

10\. Once all audiences reach the **End** element, the Progress Status will change to **Completed** within a few minutes.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*gFzV3W0hbcHj8Ty9)

11\. After confirming that the status is **Completed**, click the **Check Challenge** button again.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*p_zxG-EiiQuKejNH)

12\. If everything is evaluated correctly, the Challenge will be completed successfully.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:875/0*zKlJQtoNKpd8MyRt)

That is the current workaround.

Once Salesforce fixes the issue, these steps will likely no longer be necessary. Until then, please try completing the Challenge using the method described in this article.

title: Unlock CMS Workspaces in MC Next: 8 features you need to know
markdown:
Unlock 8 key CMS Workspace features in Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced) to optimize content, enhance collaboration, and speed up marketing workflows.

This article covers CMS workspace capabilities including content organization, collaboration tools, and workflow optimization features.

[

![François Perret #SharingIsLearning](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*7FJ72xKqml7I73XREbJYKA.png)



](https://medium.com/@francois-perret?source=post_page---byline--92a1aef23150---------------------------------------)

1 min read

Jul 17, 2025

> **_This post is a summary of the_** [**_full article_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/send-message-changes-salesforce/) **_↗️ published on The Agentic Marketer, in the_** [**_Flows & Automations_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-learning-path/flows-automations) **_Learning Path._**

The guide shows how to trigger messaging in Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced) when Salesforce records change by creating an **Engagement Signal** on the relevant DMO and handling the resulting **Custom Event** in an **Automation Event Triggered Flow**. You’ll select identifiers (user, timestamp, event), opt to count each event, optionally add criteria, then build a Flow that sends an email. Personalize the email by adding the triggering record as a **Data Source**and referencing fields via **Event Data Provider**.

- **Pattern overview:** Salesforce object change → Engagement DSO/DLO → **Engagement Signal** → Custom Event → **Automation Event Triggered Flow** → **Send Email**.
- **Key steps:** exclude related objects (so the signal is usable in flows); set user/timestamp/event identifiers; choose entry option ("every time" vs previous didn’t meet); build and activate the email-sending flow.
- **From the trenches:** Custom DMOs are supported; if you hit a "missing datagraph" error, toggle the flow’s Datagraph off/on and reactivate; avoid using the **Data Graph** for email personalization — embed needed fields in the signal instead.
- **Timing & scope:** triggering isn’t real-time (expect ~10–15 minutes); signals are for **Engagement** DMOs — Profile changes (e.g., Lead) require a transform to an Engagement DSO/DMO or a helper custom object.

👉 Read the full [**Marketing Cloud Next — Send message on Salesforce changes**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/send-message-changes-salesforce/) guide ↗️
title: Marketing Cloud Next: Send a Message When Something Happens in Salesforce

[

![François Perret #SharingIsLearning](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*7FJ72xKqml7I73XREbJYKA.png)



](https://medium.com/@francois-perret?source=post_page---byline--d1e1c2730aa2---------------------------------------)

1 min read

Apr 23, 2025

> **_This post is a summary of the_** [**_full article_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/drop-pardot-or-use-both/) **_↗️ published on The Agentic Marketer, in the_** [**_Account Engagement_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-learning-path/account-engagement) **_Learning Path._**

The guide weighs when to retain Pardot (Account Engagement), when Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced) is ready, and why a coexistence phase often makes sense before a full move.

- **When not to switch (yet):** if you depend on **Business Units**, **progressive profiling / pre-fill / Form Handlers**, **Person Accounts**, **dedicated IP** or **dynamic sender**, or specific **3rd-party integrations**, keep Pardot for now; **UTMs** exist but must be leveraged.
- **What changed (Summer ’25):** MC Next now supports **Identity licenses** (no full Salesforce license needed) and lets you **edit/import HTML emails** — two former blockers removed.
- **Why try MC Next:** gain **multichannel messaging (SMS/WhatsApp)**, **Path Experiment**, **Data Cloud segmentation** (including prompt-led), **Agentforce-built campaigns**, and **email repeaters**.
- **Practical path:** keep Pardot while you **install MC Next**: Data Cloud grants access, Pardot engagement data is **ingested to Data Cloud**, segments can flow back to **Pardot (Dynamic Lists)** and **Ads**, and **AI** investment concentrates on MC Next.

👉 Read the full [**Marketing Cloud Next: should you drop Pardot or use both?**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/drop-pardot-or-use-both/) guide ↗️
title: Marketing Cloud Next: Should You Drop Pardot or Use Both?

markdown:
[

![François Perret #SharingIsLearning](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*7FJ72xKqml7I73XREbJYKA.png)



](https://medium.com/@francois-perret?source=post_page---byline--86d37e214de8---------------------------------------)

1 min read

Apr 21, 2025

> **_This post is a summary of the_** [**_full article_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/translating-pardot-concepts/) **_↗️ published on The Agentic Marketer, in the_** [**_Account Engagement_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-learning-path/account-engagement) **_Learning Path._**

The guide maps key Pardot features to their closest equivalents (or differences) in **Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced)** — covering domains and tracking, campaigns and reporting, identity and segmentation, builders and automations, consent, attribution, AI, and licensing — so teams can plan convergence with clear expectations.

- **Foundations & assets:** Sender/Tracker Domains → Authenticated & Custom Domains; assets live in **Enhanced CMS**; Landing Pages use the standard builder.
- **Data & people:** Visitors/Prospects/Leads/Contacts are CRM + **Data Cloud** records; **Identity Resolution** creates **Unified Individuals**; segmentation uses dynamic **Segments**.
- **Automation & measurement:** Completion Actions ↔ Form/Blank Event **Flows**; **Flow Experiment** for testing; **Marketing Performance** for reporting; **Opportunity Influence** for First/Last Touch attribution.
- **Personalization & consent:** Personalisation Decisions + Variations replace Dynamic Content; **Subscription Consent** unifies channel permissions; no Form Handler today; some features remain “not available/equivalent.”
- 👉 Read the full [**Marketing Cloud Next: Translating Pardot concepts**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/translating-pardot-concepts/) guide ↗️

markdown:
Get unlimited access to the best of Medium for less than $1/week.

[

![MC Next - Deep Dives](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:76:76/1*DleVYcEoyl6dl5E5mUwXiw.png)

Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced) - Deep Dives: expert guides on Salesforce’s next-gen marketing platform built on Core for the Agentic Marketer. Learn automated flows, segmentation, consent management, personalization, reply mail management, etc. with Data Cloud and AI.

[

![François Perret #SharingIsLearning](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*7FJ72xKqml7I73XREbJYKA.png)



](https://medium.com/@francois-perret?source=post_page---byline--1dd3f51b2397---------------------------------------)

Apr 18, 2025

> **_This post is a summary of the_** [**_full article_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/subscription-consents-person-accounts/) **_↗️ published on The Agentic Marketer, in the_** [**_Segmentation & Engagement_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-learning-path/segmentation-engagement) **_Learning Path._**

The guide shows how to surface and review **subscription consents** directly on **Person Accounts** in Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced), so marketers and sales can see channel permissions at a glance and keep activation compliant.

- **Pattern overview:** expose **subscription consent** details on the Person Account record to view status per channel and key timestamps.
- **Setup notes:** add the consent component to the record page and ensure the relevant fields are visible for quick audit.
- **Usage tips:** check status and last-updated before activating campaigns; align with your DOI/consent model for consistency.
- **Why it matters:** clearer visibility, fewer mis-sends, and easier troubleshooting across teams.

👉 Read the full [**Marketing Cloud Next Subscription Consents (Person Accounts)**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/subscription-consents-person-accounts/) guide ↗️

](https://medium.com/@francois-perret?source=post_page---byline--444f0fc5351c---------------------------------------)

1 min read

Apr 14, 2025

> **_This post is a summary of the_** [**_full article_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/trigger-flow-click-open-event/) **_↗️ published on The Agentic Marketer, in the_** [**_Flows & Automations_**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-learning-path/flows-automations) **_Learning Path._**

The guide shows two ways to react to **email engagement** in Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced): handle clicks/opens inside a **Segment Triggered Flow** (using **Wait for Event**) or create a **Blank Event Flow** triggered by **Email Link Click**. It then builds a reusable **Sub Flow** to add/update **Campaign Members**, passing `IndividualId`, `CampaignId`, and `MemberStatus`, branching to **update** or **create** and distinguishing **Contact (003)** vs **Lead (00Q)**. The Blank Event Flow uses the **Event** global resource (from Email Engagement) to feed the Sub Flow.

- **Pattern overview:** Segment Triggered Flow + **Wait for Event (Click/Open)** → update Campaign status **or** Blank Event Flow (Email Link Click) → call Sub Flow to add/update Campaign Member.
- **Sub Flow essentials:** inputs (`IndividualId`, `CampaignId`, `MemberStatus`) → **Get Campaign Member** → **Decision** (exists?) → **Update** or **Create**; branch by **Contact/Lead** using ID prefixes.
- **Blank Event setup:** choose the sent **Email**, optionally restrict to **specific links**, and map the **Event.Individual** to `IndividualId`.
- **Tips:** reuse for **opens**; trigger on **every link** and route by destination (via **Resolve URL**); keep **Clicked** status even if the email is later re-opened.

👉 Read the full [**Marketing Cloud Next Click/Open Event Flow**](https://the-agentic-marketer.com/marketing-cloud-next-deep-dives/trigger-flow-click-open-event/) guide ↗️
title: Marketing Cloud Next: Trigger a Reaction on Click or Open Event

The guide explains how to set up Opportunity Influence in Marketing Cloud Next (Growth & Advanced) to credit campaigns accurately. You’ll disable legacy attribution features, activate the new First and Last Touch models via Assisted Setup, and review influenced opportunities and revenue on campaign records.

- **Pattern overview:** Disable previous Campaign Influence → run Assisted Setup for Opportunity Influence → activate First & Last Touch models.
- **Data availability:** Data appears about an hour after activation, refreshes hourly, and includes opportunities created less than 30 days ago; results are visible on the Campaign “Performance” area.
- **How it works:** Email/SMS clicks tied to a campaign (not site visits) and Contact Roles link people to opportunities; the oldest qualifying click credits First Touch, the most recent credits Last Touch.
- **Reporting notes:** Totals and influenced opportunities are switchable by model; underlying data is stored in standardized objects for reporting and dashboards.
